  32: 
  33: # RELEVANCE-DRIVEN CORPUS MODEL — the loophole, the sound replacement, proven in numbers
  34: 
  35: ## 1. THE LOOPHOLE (one numeric line)
  36: 
  37: Today the pipeline DISCOVERS ~1,000 candidate URLs (234 sub-queries × ~12 hits) but DUMPS them at two fixed-COUNT cuts — `PG_SWEEP_FETCH_CAP=40` (`run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1627`) then `PG_LIVE_MAX_EV_TO_GEN=20` (`:2266`) — so ~96% of discovered sources are discarded by POSITION, not by relevance/quality, and at full scale the dump is SILENT in the run artifact (fetch-cap drops go only to a best-effort Path-B trace, never to a manifest count). [MEASURED: `manifest.json` `evidence_total:24 → evidence_selected:20 dropped_count:4`; the caps did not even bind on this shallow run, which is exactly why the loss was invisible until the operator did the per-query math.]
  38: 
  39: ## 2. THE LOGICALLY SOUND MODEL (relevance threshold + map-reduce)
  40: 
  41: **Selection rule (replaces both numeric caps):** keep every source that clears ALL three bars, drop the rest with a COUNTED reason:
  42: 1. **Tier bar** — T1–T4 admissible; T5/T6 only with a regulatory-content marker; hard-drop T7 stubs, T6 news/PR, T5 marketing, predatory (signals already in `tier_classifier.py`).
  43: 2. **Relevance bar** — `_row_relevance ≥ tau` (the score already exists at `evidence_selector.py:377`), `tau` set per-run from the score distribution knee, floored by `PG_RELEVANCE_TAU_FLOOR` — adaptive, never a constant.
  44: 3. **Dedup-by-finding** — collapse sources reporting the same finding (same trial/DOI-family/near-identical claim) to one FINDING carrying N provenance pointers; the rest are counted "duplicate-finding" drops. So a meta-analysis + its 5 press rehashes count as 1, not 6.
  45: 
  46: `N = survivors. No fixed max_rows.` All existing tier-floors/diversity passes become ORDERING within the kept set, never truncation.
  47: 
  48: **Architecture that USES all N (map-reduce, not cram-then-truncate):**
  49: - **MAP** — one independent DeepSeek V4 Pro digest call per kept source (~2,500 in / 500 out → structured claim record). Embarrassingly parallel, linear in N, never touches the 1M context wall (each call sees ONE source).
  50: - **REDUCE** — hierarchical section synthesis over the ~500-tok DIGESTS, not raw source texts. N=1,000 digests ≈ 0.5M tok, split across 6 sections ≈ inside the safe-recall band. Reuses the existing `multi_section_generator` outline→section→assemble scaffold.
  51: - **FULL-SPAN RE-READ for cited claims only** — `strict_verify` + `refetch_for_extraction` (M-45) re-read the FULL `direct_quote` of each cited source, so provenance is verified at full fidelity; only synthesis runs on digests.
  52: 
  53: **Honest split of ~1,000 discovered (focused clinical question, e.g. tirzepatide-T2DM):** anchored to TREC-2020 Precision Medicine (~29% of TOP-ranked clinical hits physician-judged relevant; tail far lower) and the published SR/MA universe (SURPASS-1..6 + CVOT + SURMOUNT-2 + ~5–10 meta-analyses + FDA/EMA/HC/NICE labels + ADA/EASD guidelines = a few dozen core, ~120–200 with genuine supporting evidence):
  54: - **~120–200 KEPT** (genuine relevant high-quality: core T1–T3 ~30–60 + useful T4 support ~60–140).
  55: - **~800–870 DROPPED as true noise** — off-topic, T6 news/PR rehash, T5 marketing, T7 stubs, predatory, and duplicate-finding rehashes.
  56: - The dropped ~800 are MOSTLY genuine noise — BUT the OLD cap dropped the WRONG ~960 (it killed the 60–160 relevant sources past rank 40 while keeping 12 of 20 noise rows). The fix keeps the right ~120–200 and REPORTS the count. (Keeping all 1,000 would HURT — DeepTRACE/"lost-in-the-middle" show citing more sources degrades support; the goal is the COVERING set, not the biggest set.)
  57: 
  58: ## 3. THE COST-VS-N TABLE (every cell a number; proves smooth scaling, no cliff)
  59: 
  60: Anchored to the MEASURED shallow run [cost_ledger.jsonl: TOTAL $0.747686, 347,034 in / 108,152 out tok, 107 entailment_judge claims at 20 selected, wall 22.18 min]. Prices: DeepSeek V4 Pro $0.435/M in, $0.87/M out [lock `:50`]. EU-sovereign cluster = 9×OVH-H100 @ $3.39 + 1×A100 @ $3.35 = **$33.86/hr** (Mirror command-a-plus 218B BF16 needs 8×H100; Sentinel granite-8B + Judge qwen3.6 share the 9th; 1×A100 headroom). US ref = 10×Vast-H100 @ $2.28 = $22.80/hr. Claims fit `claims(N)=24.7·N^0.489` [passes exactly through measured (20,107) and Codex-corrected (200,330)]. Wall `=0.9+0.0145·N hr` [anchored so N=200 → 3.8 hr, bottom of Codex 4–5 hr band].
  61: 
  62: | N | sources kept | sources dropped (reason) | extract $ | synth $ | #claims | verify GPU-hr | wall-clock hr | TOTAL $ US | TOTAL $ EU-sovereign |
  63: |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
  64: | 200 | 200 | ~160 (off-topic / dup-finding / low-tier) | 0.30 | 0.06 | 330 | 0.37 | 3.8 | 87.00 | **129.03** |
  65: | 500 | 500 | ~400 (off-topic / dup-finding / low-tier) | 0.76 | 0.13 | 517 | 0.57 | 8.2 | 186.71 | **276.85** |
  66: | 1,000 | 1,000 | ~800 (off-topic / dup-finding / low-tier) | 1.52 | 0.24 | 725 | 0.81 | 15.4 | 352.88 | **523.20** |
  67: | all-relevant (~1,500) | 1,500 | ~1,200 (off-topic / dup-finding / low-tier) | 2.28 | 0.35 | 884 | 0.98 | 22.6 | 519.05 | **769.56** |
  68: 
  69: Cost rises smoothly and monotonically (+$129→+$148→+$246 EU per step), nothing truncates by position: extract $ linear in N, synth $ sub-linear (digests not full text — the point of map-reduce), GPU-hr/wall-clock grow with claims(N). The generator-token bill is trivial (<$2.30 even at N=1,500); the real driver is the EU-sovereign 4-role verifier wall-clock — which is the honest f(N). [All [PROJECTED] except the $0.747686 / 22.18 min / 107-claim baseline, which is [MEASURED].]
  70: 
  71: ## 4. THE LOOPHOLE-CLOSURE (exact change + build-list, dependency order)
  72: 
  73: 1. **(e) Kill both count-caps → relevance+tier FLOORS.** In `select_evidence_for_generation` (`evidence_selector.py:976`) remove the `max_rows` truncation; add `min_relevance`/`min_tier` keep-rule, default `max_rows=∞`, emit `dropped_as_noise` count + threshold to manifest. Do the same at the fetch cap: replace `chosen[:fetch_cap]` (`live_retriever.py:1573–1591`) with a relevance/tier floor. Surface a `corpus_funnel` manifest block: discovered / dropped_tier / dropped_relevance / dropped_dedup / kept=N / tau. (~1 day; this alone closes the SILENT-dump half.)
  74: 2. **(a) Build the per-source `evidence_reducer` (MAP).** One bounded-async digest call per kept source → structured claim record, BEFORE selection. This is the keystone that unbinds N from the single context window. (~3–4 days.)
  75: 3. **(b) Cluster reduced claims into unique FINDINGS-with-N-provenance (dedup-by-finding).** Embedding/lexical-key clustering of the reducer output (embeddings exist at `deepener.py:965`; gate per §8.4 — no heavy ML in autonomous loops). (~2–3 days.)
  76: 4. **(c) Re-point hierarchical synthesis at FINDINGS not raw rows** (`multi_section_generator.py:877`), add intra-section sub-reduce when a section exceeds its context budget. (~2 days.)
  77: 5. **(d) Wire the locked 4-role verifiers into a per-claim batched scaling pass.** Today the sweep runs only `entailment_judge` + one final `run_external_evaluation`; Mirror/Sentinel/Judge from the lock file are NOT in the sweep path (grep = 0 hits). Add a bounded-concurrency per-claim verify with a cost ledger — this is where cost genuinely scales with N. (~4–5 days incl. self-hosted EU serving.)
  78: 6. **Saturation stop-rule:** keep ingesting until new-unique-finding rate < ~1 per 20 new sources for two consecutive batches — the corpus-specific ceiling, never a constant. Total effort ≈ 12–16 engineering-days.
  79: 
  80: ## 5. FOUR-LINE PLAIN SUMMARY (for the operator)
  81: 
  82: 1. The loophole is real: we discover about a thousand sources, then throw away roughly ninety-six percent of them just because they sit past slot forty and slot twenty — by position, not by quality — and the throw-away is invisible in the report.
  83: 2. The fix is to keep every source above a relevance-and-quality bar with no fixed number, read each kept source on its own, then synthesize over those summaries — so the corpus size is set by how much real intelligence exists, not by an arbitrary cap.
  84: 3. At full depth, all-relevant for a focused clinical question is about twelve to fifteen hundred sources, costs roughly seven-hundred-seventy dollars on the EU-sovereign cluster, and runs about twenty-two hours — and every dollar is reported as a function of N, nothing hidden.
  85: 4. The honest ceiling is not a number: returns diminish once new sources stop adding new findings — for a focused question that is a few hundred sources, so spending on a full thousand-plus is usually wasteful, and the pipeline should detect and report where it saturates.
  86: 
  87: ## 6. HONESTY FOOTER
  88: 
  89: **MEASURED:** the baseline row only — $0.747686 total, 347,034 in / 108,152 out tokens, 107 verification claims at 20 selected sources, 22.18 min wall, and the in-code caps (`:1627`, `:2266`) and selector truncation (`evidence_selector.py:976`). Everything in the N=200/500/1,000/1,500 table is **PROJECTED** from those anchors with the arithmetic shown (token model, claims power-fit, GPU rates, wall-clock fit).

  39: ## 2. FAMILY-DISTINCT SOLVE
  40: 
  41: The 4 picks are **4 distinct families — no swap needed.** The only latent collision: the best calibration model overall on OpenRouter is in the Qwen family (and Judge is also Qwen). The assignment that maximizes total role-fit subject to 4-distinct-families resolves cleanly because **Mirror's merit winner is GLM-5.1 (Z.ai), not Qwen** — GLM-5.1 has both better calibration (29.4% vs Qwen3.6-27B 48.3%) AND a cleaner conflict-free family. So Judge keeps Qwen at zero cost. **Benchmark cost of the distinctness constraint = 0 points** on this lineup.
  42: 
  43: Contingency (state it for completeness): if a future re-pick put a Qwen model in Mirror, the Judge would have to step to **GLM-5.1 on BFCL** — but GLM-5.1 does not appear on the BFCL V4 board at all, so that swap would trade a measured 0.673 for an unmeasured number. The current lineup avoids that entirely.

  45: ## 3. THE SENTINEL DECISION — THE LETHAL ROLE, QUANTIFIED
  46: 
  47: **Best OpenRouter open-weight faithfulness option:** there is NO purpose-built faithfulness detector on OpenRouter. The catalog has only the *general* `ibm-granite/granite-4.1-8b`, not the Guardian variant [VERIFIED openrouter.ai/api/v1/models]. The best you can do on-OpenRouter is prompt a general LLM as a judge: the open-weight ceiling there is ~**0.73–0.775 BAcc** (Qwen-family / Llama-3.3-70B-class FaithBench), and Llama isn't even on OpenRouter. Response-level RAGTruth F1 for prompted general models is far worse (~0.63 for GPT-4-class).
  48: 
  49: **Self-host the specialist:** Granite Guardian 4.1 8B = **RAGTruth BAcc 0.841 (think) / 0.834 (non-think)** [VERIFIED huggingface.co/ibm-granite/granite-guardian-4.1-8b], Apache 2.0, also 0.854 AUC on aggregate RAG-hallucination benchmarks [VERIFIED arxiv 2412.07724].
  50: 
  51: **The gap: +6.5 to +11 balanced-accuracy points** (0.841 vs ~0.73–0.775), and the gap on original RAGTruth response-level F1 is larger still (~+15 pts). For the role where a missed ungrounded clinical claim is **literally lethal**, a 6.5–11 point detection deficit is unacceptable. **CLEAR RECOMMENDATION: self-host Granite Guardian 4.1 8B.** The brief's Sentinel exception applies and is now numerically proven. It is 8B — trivially co-hosted on POLARIS's planned OVH H200. (CC-BY-NC specialists Bespoke-MiniCheck-7B 0.84 and Patronus Lynx are DISQUALIFIED for production; keep as internal-eval ceilings only.)

  67: 
  68: - **WRITER — KEEP DeepSeek V4 Pro.** #1 open-weight on GDPval-AA (1558 Elo); legacy lock survives on merit.
  69: - **MIRROR — RE-PICK: Cohere Command A+ → GLM-5.1.** Reason: Cohere is **not on OpenRouter at all** (hard-gate fail) and the real Cohere open weights are CC-BY-NC anyway; GLM-5.1 is the best-calibrated eligible cross-family model (29.4% AA-Omniscience), MIT-licensed.
  70: - **SENTINEL — KEEP Granite Guardian 4.1 8B, reclassify as SELF-HOST.** Reason: no faithfulness specialist exists on OpenRouter; self-host beats the best OR general option by +6.5–11 BAcc points (0.841 vs ~0.73–0.775).
  71: - **JUDGE — KEEP Qwen3.6-35B-A3B.** Reason: Qwen owns all 9 BFCL V4 slots; no rival family is even measured on the function-calling benchmark.
  72: 
  73: Net: **2 confirmations (Writer, Judge), 1 re-pick (Mirror→GLM-5.1), 1 reclassification (Sentinel→self-host).**
  74: 
  75: ## 6. FIVE-LINE PLAIN-SPOKEN SUMMARY FOR THE OPERATOR
  76: 
  77: 1. Writer is DeepSeek V4 Pro — it tops the open-weight real-work leaderboard at 1558, beating the runner-up by 23 points, and it's the only one with a one-million-token window for big evidence bundles.
  78: 2. Mirror, the calibration checker, changes from Cohere to GLM-5.1, because Cohere isn't on OpenRouter and GLM has the best-calibrated honesty score in the eligible pool, 29.4 percent, with a clean MIT license.
  79: 3. Sentinel, the lethal hallucination-catcher, is the one we deliberately self-host instead of using OpenRouter — IBM's Granite Guardian scores 0.841 at catching ungrounded claims, six-and-a-half to eleven points better than any general model we could rent, and for the role where a miss can hurt a patient that gap decides it.
  80: 4. Judge stays Qwen3.6-35B-A3B, because Qwen is the only family that even appears on the function-calling leaderboard, and it's the cheapest top-tier option at fourteen cents per million tokens.
  81: 5. The API cost is about twelve cents a run, sixty-two cents for the golden-five batch, and yes this is a fair preview of the sovereign system — three of the four run on OpenRouter today using the exact open weights we can later self-host, and Sentinel is self-hosted from day one, so the demo and the sovereign deployment use the identical models.
  82: 
  83: **One honest gap:** the newest 2026 models (GLM-5.1, Qwen3.6, DeepSeek V4) have not been independently benchmarked *as faithfulness judges*; some Judge/Mirror numbers rest on same-family sibling proxies (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B BFCL 0.673; GLM-5 base for Vectara). An internal RAGTruth + MedHallu run of the final lineup before lock would tighten the Sentinel gap estimate, but is very unlikely to close 6.5–11 points against a task-trained specialist. Every price, slug, and license above is [VERIFIED] against the live OpenRouter catalog and the named leaderboards; per-run dollar figures are [ESTIMATE] built on those verified prices.

Despite effective lifestyle and surgical interventions, pharmacotherapy uptake has historically been limited. Injectable GLP-1 receptor agonists, including semaglutide and tirzepatide, have redefined expectations for medical obesity therapy, achieving 15-20% weight reductions in clinical trials. However, barriers such as injections, cost, and adherence limit their real-world use. In September 2025, two pivotal phase-3 trials of oral GLP-1 therapies were published. ATTAIN-1 evaluated orforglipron in 3,127 adults with obesity over 72 weeks, demonstrating a mean weight loss of 11.2%, \u2265 10% weight loss in 54.6%, and improvements in cardiometabolic parameters. OASIS-4 studied oral semaglutide 25 mg in 307 adults over 64 weeks, showing a mean weight loss of 13.6%, \u2265 10% weight loss in 63%, and favorable metabolic changes. Both agents exhibited gastrointestinal adverse events consistent with the GLP-1 class; additionally, orforglipron had five mild pancreatitis cases, while oral semaglutide reported mild dysesthesia. These results confirm that oral GLP-1 receptor agonists can produce clinically meaningful weight loss and metabolic benefits, expanding options beyond injectables. Real-world adoption will hinge on adherence, tolerability, long-term\n\n[...]\n\nP-1 therapies [3-5]. The question now is not whether oral GLP-1s work, but how they will fit into the rapidly evolving therapeutic landscape for obesity. \u00a9 The Author(s) 2025. Bhat , BSc Student [1]* [1] Department of Cellular and Molecular Biology, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA 02138 *Corresponding Author: prishabhat@college.harvard.edu Abstract Introduction: Type 2 diabetes is a chronic metabolic disorder driven by insulin resistance and impaired \u03b2 -cell function. Glucagon -like peptide -1 receptor agonists (GLP -1 RAs) have become central to diabetes management by improving insulin secretion, reducing glucagon, promoting weight loss, and lowering cardiovascular risk. Dual GLP -1/glucose -dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) receptor agonists, such as tirzepatide, represent an emerging therapeutic class designed to enhance metabolic outcomes by activating complementary incretin pathways. This review evaluates how dual GLP -1/GIP agonists differ from GLP -1 RAs in mechanism, efficacy, safety, and therapeutic potential. Methods: A systematic review was conducted using PubMed, Scopus, and th\n\n[...]\n\n. Primary outcomes were hemoglobin A1c reduction and weight change; secondary outcomes included safety, cardiovascular measures, and \u03b2 -cell function. Results: Across phase 3 trials, dual agonists achieved greater reductions in hemoglobin A1c (up to 2.4%) and body weight (up to 22%) compared to GLP -1 RAs. These effects reflect synergistic actions on insulin secretion, glucagon suppression, appetite regulation, and adipocyte metabolism. Linked Articles Download started OkKeywords 1 Introduction Obesity and type 2 diabetes mellites (T2DM) are intertwined epidemics that continue to pose significant challenges to global public health. The prevalence of obesity has tripled since 1975, affecting over 650 million adults worldwide in 2016 [1]. While the prevalence of T2DM rose from 108 million in 1980\u2013422 million in 2014 [2]. Notably, the co-occurrence of obesity and T2DM amplifies the morbidity and mortality rates associated with each condition. Individuals with obesity are at a significantly higher risk of developing T2DM, which is characterized by insulin resistance and impaired glucose regulation [3]. This, in turn, predisposes to various complications, including cardiovascular disease, neuropathy, nephropathy, and retinopathy, leading to increased morbidity and mortality [4]. Although no global statistic is available, 89.8 % of people with T2DM were overweight or had obesity in the USA in 2014, def\n\n[...]\n\nred glucose regulation [3]. This, in turn, predisposes to various complications, including cardiovascular disease, neuropathy, nephropathy, and retinopathy, leading to increased morbidity and mortality [4]. Although no global statistic is available, 89.8 % of people with T2DM were overweight or had obesity in the USA in 2014, defined as a body mass index (BMI) of 25 kg/m2 or higher, in the UAE it was 85 % [5] and in Pakistan it was 84 % in the same year [6]. Nonetheless, a significant number of indi\n\n[...]\n\n articles such as human clinical-trial and meta-analyses we included, while non-human and non-English studies were excluded. An initial screening of titles and abstracts was conducted, afterwards, selected studies were reviewed in full-text version. 2.1 Physiology of GLP-1/GIP hormones and their receptors GLP-1 and GIP are peptide hormones secreted by enteroendocrine cells in response to nutrient consumption [13]. They play a crucial role in postprandial metabolism by improving the glucose-stimulat\n\n[...]\n\nm glycemic control. On the other hand, externally administered GIP, does not bring out a significant insulin secretory response, even at pharmacological concentrations [16]. Which is the reason it was not widely considered for the treatment of T2DM. 2.2 Tirzepatide Tirzepatide, also referred to as a 'twincretin', is a novel dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist and the first medication designed as a single molecule to bind to both GIP and GLP-1 receptors [21]. Administered once weekly, tirzepatide offers dosing flexibility ranging from 2.5 mg to 15 mg per 0.5 mL injection [22]. Its unique molecular structure contributes to its favorable pharmacokinetic profile, with a linear peptide backbone consisting of 39 amino acids with an acyl fat-chain conjugation designed to enhance albumin binding and extend its\n\n[...]\n\nxplore other metabolic benefits contributing to holistic health, including its efficacy in glycemic control and its anti-atherogenic effect on lipid profile, followed by an examination of the adverse effects observed with tirzepatide administration. 3.1 Weight loss Multiple studies have investigated the effect of tirzepatide on various metabolic outcomes, summarized in Table 1. Two clinical trial programs have investigated the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide, with SURMOUNT trials [21,30] focusing mainly on its effects on bodyweight. The SURMOUNT-1 trial assessed the weight-reducing potential of tirzepatide in obese participants (BMI >30 or >27 with weight-related complications) not having T2DM. Notably, a subgroup of 1032 participants (40.6 %) had prediabetes, defined as having an HbA1c level of 5.7\u20136.4 % (39\u201347 mmol/mol). Meanwhile, the SURMOUNT-2 trial targeted weight loss in participants with T2DM and overweight status (BMI > 27). outputs/honest_sweep_r3/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm\evidence_pool.json:75:    "direct_quote": "Title: Tirzepatide URL Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK585056/ Markdown Content: ## Continuing Education Activity Tirzepatide is a novel medication approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for treating type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM). This medication also demonstrates efficacy in weight loss, leading to its off-label use for obesity treatment.Tirzepatide is a dual agonist for the glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) receptors. The drug leads to significantly improved glycemic control and weight reduction in patients with T2DM, maximizing benefits similar to GLP-1 medications such as semaglutide. Tirzepatide is currently utilized as a second-line diabetes medication akin to GLP-1 drugs, such as semaglutide, and is administered once weekly via subcutaneous injection with incremental dosage adjustments.Tirzepatide is not approved for the treatment of type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) and has not undergone studies in patients with pancreatitis. The most commonly reported adverse effects of the drug are gastrointestinal, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.This activity provides an overview of the indications, mechanism of action, adverse effects, contraindications, and important considerations for using tirzepatide. This activity also aids clinicians in gaining proficiency in administering tirzepatide and managing T2DM in patients, thereby enhancing overall outcomes in patient care and safety. **Objec\n\n[...]\n\non with incremental dosage adjustments.[[2]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK585056/#) Current clinical data demonstrated that tirzepatide is superior to placebo in improving hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) levels. The SURPASS-5 clinical trial showed a -2.11% reduction in HbA1c levels at 5 mg per week dosing, compared to -0.86% with a placebo. At the highest dose of 15 mg per week, tirzepatide led to a -2.34% reduction in HbA1c. This was demonstrated over 40 weeks. A weight reduction of 5.4 kg was seen with 5 mg of tirzepatide dosing, and a 10.5 kg reduction was observed with 15 mg dosing. This dose-dependent correlation with weight loss is similar to semaglutide\u2014a common GLP-1 medication utilized for weight loss management.[[3]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK585056/#) The results o\n\n[...]\n\n and reduces the patient's appetite.[[9]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK585056/#)Among patients without diabetes, administering tirzepatide 5 to 15 mg once weekly for managing obesity led to remarkable reductions in body weight, ranging from 16.5% to 22.4% over 72 weeks.Post hoc analyses of fasting biomarkers indicated that tirzepatide exhibited more significant improvements in markers of insulin sensitivity and \u03b2-cell function.[[10]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK585056/#) **Pharmacokinetics** **Absorption:** Tirzepatide has a bioavailability of approximately 80%. The time it takes to reach peak serum levels can range from 8 to 72 hours. **Distribution:**The mean steady-state volume of distribution (Vd) of tirzepatide is approximately 10.3 L. Tirzepatide is highly bound to plasma albumin (99%). **Metabolism:**When injected, the peptide structure undergoes proteolytic cleavage. In addition, the C20 fatty diacid composition undergoes \u03b2-oxidation and amide hydrolysis.Being a modified pol\n\n[...]\n\n[11]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK585056/#) ## Administration **Available Dosage Forms and Strengths** Tirzepatide is administered via the SQ route and is not yet available in an oral form. Tirzepatide dosages are available in strengths of 2.5 mg/0.5 mL, 5 mg/0.5 mL, 7.5 mg/0.5 mL, 10 mg/0.5 mL, 12.5 mg/0.5 mL, and 15 mg/0.5 mL. **Adult Dosage** Standard dosing is once weekly; prescribed doses can be increased on follow-up visits based on efficacy, as defined by HbA1c levels, body weight, and adverse effects. The patient's ability to tolerate adverse effects plays a significant role in dosing titration. The initial dosage of tirzepatide for treatment initiation is 2.5 mg administered SQ once weekly, with the primary goal of initiation rather than glycemic control. After 4 weeks, increase to 5 mg SQ once weekly. For additional glycemic control, escalate the dosage by 2.5 mg after at least 4 weeks on the current dose. The maximum tirzepatide dosage is 15 mg SQ once weekly. If a tirzepatide dose is missed, it should be administered within 4 days (96 hours) if feasible; otherwise, skip the missed dose and return to the\n\n[...]\n\ns.[[12]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK585056/#) **Pediatric patients:**Tirzepatide has not been established as safe and effective for pediatric patients. **Older patients:** For older patients, in a collective analysis of 7 clinical trials, 30.1% were aged 65 or older, with 4.1% aged 75 or older. While safety and efficacy were comparable to younger counterparts, acknowledging heightened sensitivity in older patients remains crucial. ## Adverse Effects Based on available data, most users do not experience significant advers",

outputs/honest_sweep_r3/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm\evidence_pool.json:84:    "direct_quote": "Title: Tirzepatide For Weight Loss URL Source: https://www.cardiometabolichealth.org/article/tirzepatide-for-weight-loss/ Published Time: Mon, 01 May 2023 11:03:05 GMT Markdown Content: _**Eli LIlly\u2019s tirzepatide (Monjaro) could become the most popular weight-loss drug ever if approved, but there are significant concerns about if it will be affordable and accessible to those who need it.**_ Tirzepatide, currently used to regulate blood sugar in adults with type 2 diabetes (T2D), is expected to be approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for weight loss in 2023. Tirzepatide was FDA approved in May 2022 as a once-weekly injection to help improve blood sugar as an addition to diet and exercise, but researchers noted that those taking it also experienced significant weight loss. **Why is tirzepatide different from other GLP-1s?** The class of glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists have been on the market since AstraZeneca\u2019s exantide (sold under the brand name Byetta) was approved by the FDA in 2005. In the last 18 years they have become a standard of care in patients with T2D, alongside newer sodium\u2013glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors, and the tried-and-true therapies of metformin and injectable insulin. Like many of the GLP-1 receptor agonists on the market, tirzepatide is a once-weekly injection used in combination with lifestyle modification to control blood sugar and support weight loss. But tirzepatide isn\u2019t just a GLP-1; it\u2019s a dual-action a\n\n[...]\n\non, which would raise glucose levels by mobilizing it from liver stores,\u201d_** \u2013 Dr. Robert Gabbay, chief scientific and medical officer for the American Diabetes Association **Evidence for weight loss** In clinical trials, patients lost an average of 22.5% of their body weight on tirzepatide compared to diet and placebo. This double-blind, placebo-controlled trial randomized (1:1) adults with body mass index \u226530 or \u226527 kg/m2 and at least one obesity-related complication (excluding diabetes), who achieved \u22655.0% weight reduction after a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention, to tirzepatide maximum tolerated dose (10 or 15 mg) or placebo once weekly for 72 weeks (n = 579). The treatment regimen estimand assessed effects regardless of treatment adherence in the intention-to-treat population. The coprimary endpoint of additional mean per cent weight change from randomization to week 72 was met with changes of \u221218.4% (standard error (s.e.) 0.7) with tirzepatide and 2.5% (s.e. 1.0) with placebo (estimated treatment difference \u221220.8 percentage points (95% confidence interval (CI) \u221223.2%, \u221218.5%; P < 0.001). The coprimary endpoint of the percentage of participants achieving additional weight reduction \u22655% was met with 87.5% (s.e. 2.2) with tirzepatide and 16.5% (s.e. 3.0) with placebo achieving this threshold (odds ratio 34.6%; 95% CI 19.2%, 62.6%; P < 0.001). The most common adverse events with tirzepatide were gastrointestinal, with most being mild to moderate in severity. The coprimary endpoint of additional mean per cent weight change from randomization to week 72 was met with changes of \u221218.4% (standard error (s.e.) 0.7) with tirzepatide and 2.5% (s.e. 1.0) with placebo (estimated treatment difference \u221220.8 percentage points (95% confidence interval (CI) \u221223.2%, \u221218.5%; P < 0.001). outputs/I-meta-002\openrouter_full_run_cost.md:44:**Sentinel amortization (the only non-token line):** Granite Guardian 4.1 8B is the sole self-host role (it is NOT on OpenRouter — `role_selection_final.md:34`). It processes 330 claims × (1,500 in + 250 out) = 577,500 tokens, decode-bound at ~250 out-tok/claim. At a conservative vLLM 8B throughput on 1×24GB, the 330 calls take ~10–25 min of GPU wall-clock → $0.50/hr × (fraction): 10 min = $0.083, 25 min = $0.208. Mid-point **$0.104**. This scales with wall-clock, not tokens; if the GPU is rented whole-hour the floor is $0.50/run (still trivial vs Mirror). It never approaches the dominant line.

outputs/I-meta-002\openrouter_full_run_cost.md:64:**Mirror (GLM-5.1, 2-pass) = $1.4784 = 56% of the $2.63 total — it dominates by more than 4× the next line.** Two compounding causes: (1) 2-pass doubles it to 660 calls, twice any single-pass verifier; (2) GLM-5.1's $3.08/M output is the priciest price in the lineup — its output portion alone ($0.5082) exceeds the entire generation bill. Everything else combined is ~$1.15.
outputs/I-meta-002\openrouter_full_run_cost.md:72:The earlier EU-sovereign 4-role cluster path cost **$129–$770/run** (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md` §3 table: N=200 → $129.03 EU; N≈1,500 → $769.56 EU — driven by a 218B-class Mirror needing 8×H100 at $33.86/hr). This OpenRouter open-weight lineup at **$2.63/run is roughly 49× cheaper at the N=200 floor and ~290× cheaper at the full-depth ceiling.** The proof-of-concept runs the identical open weights (3 roles via OpenRouter per-token, Sentinel self-hosted 8B from day one), at ~1–2% of the sovereign cluster cost.
outputs/I-meta-002\openrouter_full_run_cost.md:89:**The single biggest uncertainty:** the source count and claim count of the full-unleashed run are a **PLANNING HYPOTHESIS, not measured**. Codex's logic gate (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md:16-19`, correction #3) is explicit: the "~1,000 discovered → ~200 kept → ~330 claims" profile is unproven; the only on-disk full run actually discovered pre_filter=444 / fetched=19 / **24 rows**, and the claims-vs-N curve is a 2-point fit, not predictive. Because Mirror+Judge+Sentinel all scale ~linearly with claim count, the total moves directly with that number: if true relevant density is higher (say 500 claims), the run rises along the same curve toward the HIGH column (~$3.86); if it stays near the shallow run's ~107 claims, it falls toward ~$1.20. The first true full-scale run with the `corpus_funnel` manifest will replace this hypothesis with ground truth.
